name: Bhavachakra
shortDescription: The Bhavachakra, or Wheel of Life, is a symbolic representation of samsara, the cyclical existence of suffering and rebirth in Buddhism.
description: The Bhavachakra, also known as the Wheel of Life or Wheel of Becoming, is a profound and complex visual representation of samsara, the endless cycle of suffering and rebirth in Buddhist cosmology. It is a central teaching tool used to illustrate the principles of dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda), karma, and the nature of suffering. Typically depicted as a wheel held by Yama, the Lord of Death, the Bhavachakra is divided into several concentric layers. At the center are three animals representing the 'three poisons' that drive the cycle: a pig (ignorance), a rooster (attachment), and a snake (aversion). The second layer depicts beings moving upwards towards a better rebirth and downwards towards a worse one, illustrating the workings of karma. The third layer shows the six realms of existence: gods, demigods, humans, animals, hungry ghosts, and hell beings. These realms represent different states of consciousness and potential rebirths. The outer rim of the wheel displays the twelve links of dependent origination, a detailed explanation of the causal chain that leads to suffering. Understanding the Bhavachakra is crucial for comprehending the Buddhist path to liberation, as it highlights the impermanence of all phenomena and the need to break free from the cycle of suffering through wisdom and compassion. It is closely associated with the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path, providing a framework for understanding the nature of suffering and the path to its cessation. The Bhavachakra serves as a constant reminder of the impermanent and unsatisfactory nature of existence, motivating practitioners to cultivate mindfulness, ethical conduct, and wisdom in order to achieve enlightenment.

sources.title: The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism
sources.author: Robert E. Buswell Jr. and Donald S. Lopez Jr.

sources.title: The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
sources.author: Thich Nhat Hanh
